content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 4,
1993
Daniel S.
Hentschke
Interim City Attorney
City of Oceanside
300 North Hill Street
Oceanside,
CA
92054
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—660
Dear Mr.
Hentschke:
This is in response to your request for informal assistance
on behalf of Oceanside City Councilmember Colleen Richardson
O’Harra under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
!
QUESTION
Is
a person,
including a business entity,
who is a source of
income to
a
law firm which employs Councilmember O’Harra,
also a
source of income to Councilmember O’Harra?
CONCLUS ION
Both the
law firm and the clients of Councilmember O’Harra
are sources of
income to Councilmember O’Harra.
FACTS
Councilmember Colleen Richardson O’Harra was elected to the
Oceanside City Council in November 1992.
She practices law with
the
law firm of Gore,
Grosse,
Greenman
& Lacy.
She is not a
partner in the firm.
According to your letter,
her relationship
with the firm is unique among the lawyers in that firm.
Unlike
other associates
in the firm who receive
a salary plus
a potential
bonus from firm profits,
Councilmember O’Harra is compensated
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section
18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
Informal
assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity
provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code
Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1—92—660
Page Two
based upon a percentage of the gross
income received solely from
her billings.
Although she is guaranteed a certain base salary,
the compensation based upon the percentage of the gross income
from her billings has always been more than the base salary.
She
does not share in the profits of the firm.
You indicated by telephone that you believed the arrangement
was premised by her pre—existing law practice.
You were not sure
if her clients were referred to her by the firm,
or were self—
generating.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable
2
! that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
2!
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However,
if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
1-92-660
Page Three
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
Section 87103.
Accordingly,
any person or business that has made any
payments to Councilmember O’Harra is a source of income to her.
If,
in the aggregate,
the payments totalled $250 or more in the
past
12 months,
the source is
a potentially disqualifying
financial interest for the purposes of Section 87103.
In your letter you stated that Councilmember O’Harra is not a
salaried employee of the firm,
but receives
a percentage of her
gross billings.
The firm retains the remainder.
Under these
facts, it appears that both the firm and her clients are jointly
responsible for the income she receives.
(Schenk Advice Letter
A—
90-460;
See also In re Carey
(1977)
3
FPPC Ops.
99,
copy
enclosed.)
Thus,
Councilmember O’Harra is required to disclose income
from any source aggregating $250 or more.
In addition,
she may
not participate in any decision that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the source of income.
If you have any questions,
or need to discuss this further,
please feel free to contact the Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Coupsel
/‘
//
/
/
By
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JN/JET/jt
Enclosures

a person,

who is a source of
income to
